evidence_quotes: I received a text message from Bank of America asking if I attempted to make a purchase for {$170.00} Krogers with my credit card on XX/XX/XXXX at XXXX.
He then asked if I knew a XXXX XXXX. He asked if I was trying to wire her {$5500.00}.
He then proceeded to tell me I would likely be on hold with Bank of America for at least 40 minutes and would miss my chance to cancel the wire.
I then asked to speak to a manager to ensure this was bank of America.
He then transferred me to a man who claimed to be XXXX XXXX.
He walked me though step by step on my account and informed me that I was submitting a wire reversal.
I called Bank of America the next day when I didn't receive the letter. They said they would send it again and give it 24 hours.
I called the following day and spoke to someone in the fraud department who advised me to go to a local branch and they could send the document to the branch.
I called almost every day for 3 weeks to get updates about my claim.

I called a few days later again and was told the same thing but that sometimes this process takes some time because XXXX XXXX might have to request the money from another bank if my money was transferred from that account to an account outside of XXXX XXXX.
parsed_response.rationale: The narrative describes a fraudulent phone call and wire transfer attempt, but there is no mention of digital asset engagement, sustained investing, crypto activity, or repeated online asset activity. The individual was involved in a wire transfer, but it was part of a scam, not a legitimate financial activity. The narrative does not indicate any online financial activity beyond the fraudulent wire transfer.
